coredata.dc:title: Electrochemistry and electrochemiluminescence of resorufin dye: Synergetic reductive-oxidation boosted by hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Seo, Young Hun
Jeon, Sang Hyeon
Shim, Beom-Jun
Shin, Ik-Soo
coredata.dc:description: To develop robust field diagnostic systems capable of responding to medical needs, miniaturization of devices and high target sensitivity remain significant challenges. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an ideal analysis tool to meet these challenges, and developing novel ECL materials is essential. Here we report the reversible electrochemical properties of resorufin, a commercial fluorescent dye, and its highly stable cathodic ECL. We find the cathodic ECL emission from the resorufin quantitatively changes upon the H2O2 content and propose the selective ECL molecular sensor against it. Resorufin undergoes reversible two-electron-transfer electrochemical reduction, and it generates cathodic ECL via a reductive-oxidation pathway with the S2O8 2− co-reactant. Interestingly, highly enhanced, synergetic ECL is produced in the dual K2S2O8–H2O2 co-reactant system, and especially, it can be used for selective molecular recognition of H2O2. Via the chronoamperometric application, the resorufin ECL signal reveals the linear correlation with the H2O2 concentration range of 0–150 µM with a detection limit of 6.8 µM. Resorufin exhibits strong potential as a new ECL luminophore. Also, selective enhancement of ECL suggests potential applicability to various immunoassays using H2O2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemistry
Electrochemiluminescence
Resorufin
Dual co-reactant
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the research grants from National Research Foundation of Korea ( 2017M3A9D8029943 and 2019K1A4A7A02102530 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of (left) the redox characteristics of resorufin, and (right) the cathodic ECL generation in a single or a dual co-reactant route (inset: (i) electrochemical reduction, (ii) chemical oxidation).

(a) Cyclic voltammogram (CV) of 1 mM resorufin (pink) in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) and CB without resorufin (gray) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s (the arrows indicate the scanning direction). WE: glassy carbon, CE: Pt wire, RE: Ag/AgCl. (b) Comparison of simulated (dark gray) and experimental (red) reduction peak of 1 mM resorufin in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

(a) CVs and (b) the corresponding ECL profiles of 0.1 mM resorufin in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) containing 10 mM H2O2 (red), 10 mM K2S2O8 (orange) and no co-reactant (gray) (scan rate: 0.1 V /s), (c) the ECL emission profile from 0.1 mM resorufin and 10 mM H2O2 in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) under the repetitive PA between 0 ∼ −0.9 V (duration: 1 s). (d) CVs and (e) the corresponding ECL profiles of 0.1 mM resorufin with 10 mM TPrA (blue) or without co-reactant (gray) (scan rate: 0.1 V/s) (f) ECL emission profile from 0.1 mM resorufin in and 10 mM TPrA under PA between 0 ∼ 0.9 V (duration: 1 s).

(a) ECL profiles of 0.1 mM resorufin in 0.05 M CB of different pH values (9, 9.5, 10, 10.5, 11, 11.5, 12) containing 10 mM K2S2O8 (orange), 10 mM H2O2 (red), and 10 mM K2S2O8–10 mM H2O2 (violet). (b) ECL profiles of 0.1 mM resorufin upon the addition of various concentrations of H2O2 (0–20 mM) in 0.05 M CB with a K2S2O8 concentration of 0.01 M (black), 0.05 M (wine), and 0.1 M (red).

(a) ECL intensity-time profiles of 0.1 mM resorufin in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) upon the addition of various concentrations of H2O2 (0, 20, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150 µM) pulsed at −0.9 V, −0.8 V, and −0.7 V (pulse duration: 1 s). (b) Integrated ECL intensity of resorufin upon titration with H2O2 (0–150 µM) in a solution of 0.05 M CB (pH 10) and 10 mM K2S2O8 pulsed at −0.7 V. (c) ECL intensity of 0.1 mM resorufin in the presence of 150 µM H2O2 and other interferences—ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), and uric acid (UA)—in a solution of 0.05 M CB (pH 10) and 10 mM K2S2O8.

head.ce:title: Electrochemistry and electrochemiluminescence of resorufin dye: Synergetic reductive-oxidation boosted by hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Group, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Europe, Campus E7.1, Saarbrücken 66123, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology Europe

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aBiosensor Group, Korea Institute of Science and Technology Europe, Campus E7.1, Saarbrücken 66123, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Soongsil University, 369 Sangdo-Ro, Dongjak-Gu, Seoul 06978, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Soongsil University, 369 Sangdo-Ro, Dongjak-Gu, Seoul 06978, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To develop robust field diagnostic systems capable of responding to medical needs, miniaturization of devices and high target sensitivity remain significant challenges. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an ideal analysis tool to meet these challenges, and developing novel ECL materials is essential. Here we report the reversible electrochemical properties of resorufin, a commercial fluorescent dye, and its highly stable cathodic ECL. We find the cathodic ECL emission from the resorufin quantitatively changes upon the H2O2 content and propose the selective ECL molecular sensor against it. Resorufin undergoes reversible two-electron-transfer electrochemical reduction, and it generates cathodic ECL via a reductive-oxidation pathway with the S2O8 2− co-reactant. Interestingly, highly enhanced, synergetic ECL is produced in the dual K2S2O8–H2O2 co-reactant system, and especially, it can be used for selective molecular recognition of H2O2. Via the chronoamperometric application, the resorufin ECL signal reveals the linear correlation with the H2O2 concentration range of 0–150 µM with a detection limit of 6.8 µM. Resorufin exhibits strong potential as a new ECL luminophore. Also, selective enhancement of ECL suggests potential applicability to various immunoassays using H2O2.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an electrochemically activated light emission generated on the electrode surface through a redox reaction [1,2]. Since ECL was first discovered in the 1960s, the demand for this key technology for the precise analysis of target analytes for biological applications [3–6], clinical diagnosis [7–9], environmental assessment [10–12], and food safety analysis [13–16] has gradually increased. ECL is advantageous because of its ultrasensitive sensing through nearly zero-background signals and it can be used to build a portable diagnostic device. Among the various types of ECL luminophores, Ru(II) and Ir(III) metallic complexes exhibit high ECL efficiency, and thus demonstrate good functionality as emitters for immune-sensors [17–19] and chemosensors [20–25]. Metal-free organic luminophores such as luminol [26–28], BODIPY [29,30], π-conjugated molecules [31–33], and carbon quantum dots [34–36] are low-cost representative candidates for ECL applications. Recently, luminophores that exhibit thermal activation delayed fluorescence (TADF) or aggregation-induced emission (AIE) through structural deformation have been attracting attention as ideal materials [37–40] for ECL applications. Continued interest in new ECL materials will promote practical ECL applications.

Among organic fluorophores, resorufin dye has excellent photophysical properties (high photoluminescence quantum yield and high extinction coefficient) and high biocompatibility (water solubility) [41]. Easy modification of resorufin structures to achieve unique spectroscopic features has successfully led to the commercialization of such materials, for example, Amplex™ Red, which demonstrates the wide applicability of resorufin derivatives [42–44]. In addition, resorufin-based studies in electrochemical analysis and sensing [45–47] have been reported; however, few studies have been conducted to investigate the ECL properties of resorufin. However, until now, the ECL emission potential of resorufin, which has good optical and electrochemical characteristics, has not been verified yet.

By the way, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a reactive oxygen species (ROS), is an intermediate in various metabolic pathways and decomposes into water and oxygen molecules by the action of peroxidase. Excess amounts of H2O2 in the human body are converted into toxic hydroxyl radicals via the Fenton reaction, which promotes oxidative stress, thereby causing inflammatory diseases and cancer [48]. The H2O2 level in human urine and blood is an excellent biomarker of oxidative stress, and can be detected via electrochemical, colorimetric, fluorescence, and ECL optical analyses [49–52]. Luminol/H2O2 is a well-known luminophore/co-reactant pair for ECL analyses and is most commonly used for H2O2 detection owing to its low cost, low oxidation potential, and bright luminescence [53]. Despite these advantages, luminol has difficulty in stable ECL signaling in repeated cycles owing to the irreversible oxidation. Therefore, a suitable ECL luminophore for H2O2 with improved detection reliability of the ECL signal is required.

Herein, we report the ECL signal of resorufin and the corresponding ECL protocol for the successful detection of H2O2. We investigated the ECL properties of resorufin based on its electrochemical behavior and simulation studies. The reversible reduction peak of resorufin contributed to the generation of stable ECL signals via the cathodic ECL pathway (Scheme 1 ). The ECL signal of resorufin observed in a single K2S2O8 co-reactant system became stronger in the dual co-reactant system with H2O2 as the co-reactant. Resorufin, which generated H2O2-sensitive ECL signals, can quantitatively recognize H2O2 under a single K2S2O8 co-reactant system through the chronoamperometry (CA) application. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to report the ECL signal from resorufin for the sensitive detection of H2O2.

Instrumentation and Materials. All chemicals used were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corp., (MO, USA). DI water was obtained using the Aquapuri 5 series water purification system (Young-In, Seoul, Korea). 0.05 M carbonate buffer (CB) pH 10 was prepared by adding appropriate amounts of sodium bicarbonate and sodium carbonate to distilled water. CB was adjusted to pH 10 by 10 M KOH or 37% HCl solution. 1 mM resorufin stock and sample solutions were freshly prepared in CB. Electrochemical measurements were performed using CH Instruments 660D Electrochemical Analyzer (CH Instruments, Inc., TX, USA). A regular three-electrode system was used in which a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter), Ag/Ag+ electrode, and platinum wire were used as the working electrode (WE), reference electrode (RE), and counter electrode (CE), respectively. GCE was polished using 0.05 µm alumina (Buehler, IL, USA), following which it was sonicated in DI water:ethanol (1:1 v/v) for 5 min and dried in nitrogen gas for 1 min. ECL signals were obtained via a portable ECL platform consisting of a photomultiplier tube (PMT, H10682–210, Hamamatsu, Tokyo, Japan) and potentials were applied to the sample by cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA) or pulsed amperometry (PA) via a connected potentiates. Screen-printed electrodes (SPEs, Model: 110, Dropsens, Spain)—carbon ink WE and CE and Ag RE were housed in a removable cell. ECL data were collected simultaneously using the PMT and the potential was controlled by the potentiostat DY2322 (Digi-Ivy, TX, USA). SPEs were discarded after each experiment.

Electrochemical Measurements. The CV of 1 mM resorufin in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) was obtained by scanning the system negatively from 0 to −0.9 V and positively from 0 to 0.9 V at a scan rate of 0.05 V/s. To gain insights into the reversible reduction peak of resorufin, CV experiments with 0.25 mM resorufin in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) at scan rates ranging from 0.01 V/s to 0.5 V/s were performed. The plot of the peak current as a function of the square root of scan rate was generated based on the Randles–Sevcik equation. The CA experiments were performed in a potential region where no Faradaic reactions occur by applying a small potential (50 mV) to calculate the uncompensated resistance (R u) and electric double layer capacitance (C d) of the system. Subsequently, a plot of the natural logarithm of the current as a function of time was plotted to calculate R u and C d. Using the uncompensated resistance and double layer capacitance values, the CV simulation was performed for the reduction of resorufin following an EE mechanism via DigiSim 3.03 software (Bioanalytical Systems, Inc) to compare the simulated and experimental reduction waves for 1 mM resorufin in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) at different scan rates from 0.01 V/s to 10 V/s.

Optimization of the ECL Response to Dual Co-reactants. K2S2O8 (0.2 M) and resorufin (1 mM) stock solutions were made freshly in 0.05 M CB (pH 10). H2O2 (1.958 mM) stock solution was prepared in DI water. Tri-n-propylamine (TPrA, 5.15 M) stock solution was used as purchased. All sample solutions were prepared in the same buffer. In pH-dependent experiments, HCl (37%) and NaOH (10 M) were used to adjust the pH of the solution from pH 9 to 12. The 914 pH/Conductometer (Metrohm AG, Switzerland) was used for pH measurements. ECL measurements were performed by scanning the potential applied to the cathodic co-reactant systems using CV from 0 to −1.6 V with a scan rate of 0.1 V/s. The anodic ECL system with TPrA was scanned from 0 to 1.4 V with a scan rate of 0.1 V/s. For detection experiments, CA method was used instead of CV, with 6 alternating pulses of 1 s at 0 V and −0.7 V.

ECL molecular sensing for H2O2. PA was chosen as the electrochemical technique for the ECL molecular sensing for H2O2 using resorufin. The testing samples were pulsed at a potential region in which the difference in the ECL signal in the presence and absence of H2O2 is most significant. Different potentials were evaluated to determine the potential that yielded the best linearity between ECL response and H2O2 concentration. Resorufin (1 mM) and 0.2 M K2S2O8 stock solutions were prepared in 0.05 M CB (pH 10). H2O2 (1.958 mM) stock solution was prepared in DI water. The samples for the detection experiments contained 0.1 mM resorufin, 0.05 M K2S2O8 and different concentrations (0–300 µM) of H2O2 in 0.1 M CB (pH 10). Common interfering species (ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Stock solutions (0.2 M) of ascorbic acid and dopamine were prepared in DI water. Stock solution (0.02 M) of uric acid was prepared in 0.05 M CB (pH 10). Selectivity tests were performed by measuring the ECL response of samples containing 0.1 mM resorufin and 0.05 M K2S2O8 with 200 µM of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), and H2O2 in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) when pulsed at −0.70 V.

Fig. 1 a shows the electrochemical behavior of resorufin which shows the reduction in the negative range and oxidation in the positive range. The CV of resorufin was measured in 0.05 M CB (pH 10) under a nitrogen atmosphere. Resorufin exhibits an irreversible oxidation around 0.65 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), whereas a quasi-reversible reduction was observed at E 1/2(red) = −0.47 V with a peak-to-peak separation (ΔE p) of ∼49 mV. Resorufin is known to convert into dihydroresorufin (DH) by a fast quasi-reversible two-electron transfer reduction. According to the Randles–Sevcik analysis, the reduction current increases linearly with the square root of the scan rate (ν 1/2) (Fig. S1), indicating that the reduction reaction is diffusion-limited [54,55]. The diffusion coefficient of resorufin is calculated as 5.17 × 10−6 cm2/s, which is consistent with the values reported in the literature [45].

A CA experiment was performed in a potential region where no Faradaic reactions occur by applying a small potential (50 mV) to calculate the uncompensated resistance (R u) and double layer capacitance (C d) of the system (Fig. S2). An electric double layer is formed when ions in the solution adhere to the surface of the electrode. A plot of the natural logarithm of current vs. time demonstrates good linearity, the slope and intercept of which are used to calculate Ru and Cd with the following equation:
(1)
ln
(
i
( t )
)
= ln
(
Δ
E
R u
)
−
t
R u
C d
R u and C d values are calculated to be 1053 Ω and 8.5 × 10−7 F, respectively.

Using R u and C d values, the CV simulation was performed for the reduction of resorufin by following a two-electron transfer EE mechanism with the typical rate constant as 80,000 (k 1°) and 320,000 (k 2°) cm/s, respectively (the area of the electrode is 0.069 cm2) (Fig. 2b and Fig. S3). The half-peak potentials of the two-electron transfer reactions have similar values (i.e., −0.46 and −0.49 V). Experimental and simulated data in CV were consistent with one another across the various scan rates, which confirms two-electron heterogenous transfer reduction of resorufin. The reversible electrochemical behavior of a luminophore is one of the critical requirements in ECL generation, and thus it suggests that resorufin has the possibility for the reductive oxidation ECL route through the reduction reaction.

Based on the electrochemical results, we investigated the ECL emission properties of resorufin via the co-reactant pathway. Its reversible reduction implies that it would show efficient cathodic ECL via the reductive-oxidation route. Thus, we measured the cathodic ECL of resorufin with each co-reactant, 10 mM H2O2 and K2S2O8, respectively. Fig. 2 a and 2b show the CVs and the corresponding ECL curves of resorufin with each co-reactant. The resorufin/H2O2 system generates the ECL signal in the potential range of −0.4 to −1.6 V. The emission intensity gradually increases with the H2O2 concentration (Fig. S4a). Although H2O2 can be fully reduced to H2O beyond the OH· radical generation in the potential range of −1.1 V to −1.6 V, ECL from resorufin is observed to increase with the H2O2 concentration even in this region. The resorufin/K2S2O8 system also exhibits ECL in the same potential region (−0.4 to −1.6 V), increasing emission intensity with K2S2O8 (Fig. S4b). In Fig. 2a, a weak ECL is also produced in resorufin alone without any co-reactant, presumably caused by hydroxide radical during the electrochemical oxygen reduction. Although the two systems exhibit similar ECL behaviors, the resorufin/H2O2 system demonstrates four times stronger emission intensity than that of the resorufin/K2S2O8 in comparison. It seems similar to that of the luminol/H2O2 system, but unlike luminol, resorufin undergoes reversible cathodic ECL, which enables a more stable and reliable emission signal. Therefore, the resorufin/H2O2 system has the potential to be used as an alternative in various ECL analysis based on the luminol/H2O2 system. The presence of resorufin does not cause any noticeable change in the negative CV waves from the solution (Fig. S5), but an apparent ECL signal is only observed when resorufin is present in the CB buffer, which clearly shows that resorufin is the key luminophore generating cathodic ECL with the two co-reactants (Fig. S6). The ECL spectral result shows a broad emission peaking around 600 nm, which is similar to that of its photoluminescence (Fig. S7).

The reversible electrochemical reduction of resorufin implies that it is structurally stable and regenerated during the cathodic ECL process. As seen in Fig. 2c, the PA between 0 V and −0.9 V shows a stable and reproducible ECL signal during repeated application of the ECL process. We gave pulsed potential at −0.9 V in order to avoid the full reduction process of H2O into H2O2. By the way, although resorufin shows irreversible oxidation behavior, we observed whether the anodic oxidative process with the TPrA co-reactant exhibits anodic ECL behavior. As shown in Fig. 2d and 2e, resorufin shows to generate the anodic ECL signal with 10 mM TPrA along with an increase in an oxidation current of TPrA in a potential range of 0.4–1.3 V, but the emission intensity gradually decreases in a repeating cycle of the anodic ECL process Fig. 2f). This result suggests that resorufin undergoes a degradable ECL due to its irreversible anodic oxidation. In addition, to confirm whether resorufin can reversibly undergo between its reduced and neutral form during the cathodic ECL process, we measured the changes in fluorescent spectrum of the resorufin during successive chemical treatment for reduction and re-oxidation. Instead of electrochemical method, we chemically reduced resorufin by adding glucose, and then added K2S2O8 to the solution again to re-oxidize the reduced form of resorufin. Before the addition of glucose, resorufin itself exhibits a strong fluorescent spectrum. However, when it is reduced to glucose, its fluorescence is significantly reduced, and then it restores the fluorescence after the addition of K2S2O8, an oxidizing agent and the cathodic ECL co-reactant (Fig. S8). Based on these results, the cathodic ECL mechanism of resorufin with H2O2 (Eq. (2)–(5) and K2S2O8 Eq. (6)–(9) can be suggested as follows.
i) Cathodic ECL mechanism in the presence of H2O2
(2) H2O2 + e− → OH– + OH·
(3) Resorufin + 2e−+ 2H+ → dihydroresorufin (DH)
(4) 2OH· + DH−→ resorufin* + 2H2O
(5) Resorufin* → resorufin + hν
ii) Cathodic ECL mechanism in the presence of K2S2O8
(6) S2O8 2– + e− → SO4 ·– + SO4 2–
(7) Resorufin + 2e−+ 2H+ → dihydroresorufin (DH)
(8) 2SO4 ·– + DH−→ resorufin* + 2SO4 2–
(9) Resorufin* → resorufin + hν

Interestingly, we observed that the ECL signal intensity further boosts when the two co-reactants of H2O2 and K2S2O8 are present together with resorufin. Previously reported studies suggest that the ECL signal of a luminophore could be improved by the synergetic effect of dual co-reactants, thereby facilitate sensitive analysis for target biomarkers [56,57]. According to these, in our study, the dual co-reactant ECL system also seems to promote the generation of SO4 ·– and OH· to amplify the ECL generation from resorufin.

We carefully observed the cathodic ECL of resorufin in dual co-reactant system and then found that it could be utilized for the highly selective and sensitive ECL molecular sensing system for H2O2. First, we found the optimized pH condition for cathodic ECL of each co-reactant system. In Fig. 3 a, the ECL signal intensity enhances as increasing pH, with the strongest signal at pH 10.5. On the other hand, in the resorufin/H2O2 system, ECL emission shows the strongest at pH 9.5, following which the signal gradually decreases at higher pH values; this can be attributed to the decomposition of H2O2 at higher pH [58,59]. Notably, the ECL intensity in the dual co-reactant system shows the strongest at pH 10, and the overall ECL behavior presents similar to that of resorufin/H2O2 system. Next, we monitored the change in the ECL signal of resorufin/ K2S2O8 system in the various H2O2 concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3b, ECL signal increases with the H2O2 concentration, which suggests that OH· formed by H2O2 dramatically promotes the SO4 ·– concentration during electrochemical decomposition of K2S2O8 [56]. Interestingly, unlike previously reported results, no significant signal reduction is observed owing to excess amount of H2O2 present in the resorufin/K2S2O8 system. In addition, this ECL signal enhancement is reliably observed during repetitive cycling through the pulsed amperometric (PA) application between 0 V and −0.9 V (Fig. S9). The ECL molecular sensing mechanism of H2O2 by the resorufin/K2S2O8 system can be proposed as follows:
iii) Synergetic ECL in the presence of K2S2O8 and H2O2
(10) SO4 ·– + H2O → OH· + HSO4 –
(11) OH· + S2O8 2– → SO4 ·– + HSO4 – + 1/2O2
(12) Resorufin + 2e−+ 2H+ → dihydroresorufin (DH)
(13) 2SO4 ·– (excess) + DH−→ resorufin* + 2SO4 2–
(14) Resorufin* → resorufin + hν

Synergetic ECL boosting in the dual H2O2/K2S2O8 co-reactant system is known in several previous papers. Especially, the Chen group proved well through the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments that OH· from the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 promotes an increase in SO4 −· from S2O8 2− [56]. The EPR spectra in Fig. S10 show the hyperfine splitting constants of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) radical adducts in different systems. Briefly, in Fig. S10, we found the addition of 10 mM H2O2 (curve a), does not significantly enhance the DMPO−OH adduct signal in 0.05 CB containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 (curve b). This is because the synergistic effect would consume the OH• formed by electrochemical reduction (eq (11)).

The above results indicate that the amplified ECL by the synergistic effect of the dual co-reactants system can be adapted to ECL molecular sensing for H2O2. Fig. 4 a shows the cathodic ECL of resorufin/dual co-reactant system under the application of different pulsing voltages. Although the strongest ECL signal is shown in the −0.9 V pulse application, it is observed that the −0.7 V application is the most beneficial in terms of quantitative change in the signal upon the addition of H2O2. The overall ECL signal weakens with the decrease in pulsing voltage, but H2O2 is successfully monitored by minimizing the interference ECL signal. The variation of ECL intensities displays a good linear relationship with the H2O2 concentration in the range 20–150 µM with a detection limit of 6.7 µM pulsed at −0.7 V, where few interference signals appear (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c compares the ECL intensity of resorufin/K2S2O8 generated when H2O2 and other interfering substances (ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid) are added respectively. The intensity against H2O2 is approximately 2.8 times higher than the others. Therefore, these results demonstrate the potential applicability of the resorufin ECL in a various biosensing fields related to H2O2.

In this study, we report electrochemistry of resorufin and its efficient ECL in various co-reactant systems. Resorufin undergoes electrochemical oxidation and reduction in the range of 0.9 V ∼ −0.9 V. Especially, it exhibits reversible two-electron reduction, and correspondingly strong and reliable ECL with the cathodic co-reactants such as H2O2 and K2S2O8, respectively. The ECL signal intensity from each co-reactant system can be further amplified via a dual co-reactant approach which presumably contributes to an increased production of SO4 ·–. The amplified ECL signal of resorufin/K2S2O8 system can be successfully adapted for molecular sensing of H2O2 with achieving higher selectivity and sensitivity together. We expect that our findings will promote the potential applications of resorufin as new ECL luminophore for bioanalysis in clinical diagnosis, environmental assessment, and food safety evaluation.
The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Young Hun Seo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Sang Hyeon Jeon: . Beom-Jun Shim: . Ik-Soo Shin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the research grants from National Research Foundation of Korea (2017M3A9D8029943 and 2019K1A4A7A02102530).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.141432.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Li, Y. Chen, J.J. Zhu, Recent advances in Electrochemiluminescence analysis, Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 358–371, 10.1021/acs.analchem.6b04675

sb:title.sb:maintitle: U1 snRNP regulates cancer cell migration and invasion in vitro

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.M. Oh, C.C. Venters, C. Di, A.M. Pinto, L. Wan, I. Younis, Z. Cai, C. Arai, B.R. So, J. Duan, G. Dreyfuss, U1 snRNP regulates cancer cell migration and invasion in vitro, Nat. Commun. 11 (2020) 1, 10.1038/s41467-019-13993-7

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single cell electrochemiluminescence imaging: from the proof-of-concept to disposable device-based analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Valenti, S. Scarabino, B. Goudeau, A. Lesch, M. Jović, E. Villani, M. Sentic, S. Rapino, S. Arbault, F. Paolucci, N.
